questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, which case will be invoked when we insert node 50 into the tree?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes.

Tree Structure Overview:
- Root: **30** (Black)
- Left child of 30: **15** (Black)
- Right child of 30: 45 (Red)
- Left child of 45: **35** (Black)
- Right child of 45: **60** (Black)
- Left child of 60: 55 (Red)
questions.explanation:
To insert 50: 30 → 45 → 60 → 55 → 50 (inserted as left child of 55).
1. Node 50 is Red.
2. Parent 55 is Red (violation).
3. Uncle of 55 is the right child of 60, which is NIL (Black).
4. Grandparent is 60. The configuration is a 'line' (55 is Left child of 60, 50 is Left child of 55).
In standard Red-Black tree algorithms (like CLRS), when the parent is red, the uncle is black, and they form a line, it is **Case 3**. This requires a rotation and recoloring.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, which case will be invoked when we insert node 10 into the tree?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes.

Tree Structure Overview:
- Root: **30** (Black)
- Left child of 30: **15** (Black)
- Right child of 30: 45 (Red)
- Left child of 45: **35** (Black)
- Right child of 45: **60** (Black)
- Left child of 60: 55 (Red)
questions.explanation:
To insert 10: 30 → 15 → 10 (inserted as left child of 15).
The newly inserted node (10) is Red. Its parent, node **15**, is **Black**. Because a Red node can have a Black parent without violating Red-Black tree properties (specifically Property 4), no rebalancing cases are triggered. The answer is 'none'.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the height of the tree?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes.

Tree Structure Overview:
- Root: **30** (Black)
- Left child of 30: **15** (Black)
- Right child of 30: 45 (Red)
- Left child of 45: **35** (Black)
- Right child of 45: **60** (Black)
- Left child of 60: 55 (Red)
questions.explanation:
In Red-Black trees, NIL nodes are treated as the leaves of the tree. The height of the tree is defined as the length of the longest path from the root to any NIL leaf node.
Path 1: 30 → 15 → NIL (length 2)
Path 2: 30 → 45 → 60 → 55 → NIL (length 4)
The longest path has 4 edges, so the height is 4.

questions.text: The array \{25, 18, 19, 17, 14, 16, 10, 13, 21, 5, 12, 11, 7\} is not a max-heap, but the max-heap properties are violated only once (involving numbers). Which two numbers (parent and child) in the array violate the heap properties?
questions.explanation:
By checking the max-heap property (Parent \( \ge \) Children) using 1-based indexing:
- Parent index 1 (25): Children 18, 19 (Valid)
- Parent index 2 (18): Children 17, 14 (Valid)
- Parent index 3 (19): Children 16, 10 (Valid)
- Parent index 4 (**17**): Children 13, **21**. Since 17 < 21, the property is violated.
The violating pair is 17 (parent) and 21 (child).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, list the keys of the nodes using the pre-order traversal.
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nill nodes.

Tree Structure Overview:
- Root: **30** (Black)
- Left child of 30: **15** (Black)
- Right child of 30: 45 (Red)
- Left child of 45: **35** (Black)
- Right child of 45: **60** (Black)
- Left child of 60: 55 (Red)
questions.explanation:
Pre-order traversal visits the current node, then the left subtree, then the right subtree (Root-Left-Right).
1. Visit Root: **30**
2. Visit Left child: **15**
3. Visit Right subtree: **45** → Left child **35** → Right subtree of 45 starting with **60** → Left child of 60 **55**.
Result: 30, 15, 45, 35, 60, 55.

Both functions are constants. In asymptotic analysis, all constant functions belong to the same complexity class \( \Theta(1) \). Therefore, \( f(n) \) is simultaneously Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta of \( g(n) \).

questions.text: Given the array A \( <11, 10, 1, 6, 4, 13, 9, 7> \) what are the two sub arrays generated after applying the partition procedure in the Quick sort algorithm one time :
questions.explanation:
Using the Lomuto partition with 7 as the pivot:
1. Elements smaller than 7 (1, 6, 4) are moved to the left.
2. Elements larger than 7 (11, 10, 13, 9) are moved to the right.
3. The pivot 7 is placed in its correct sorted position.
The state of the array after partitioning is `[1, 6, 4, 7, 11, 13, 9, 10]`. The subarrays generated are `[1, 6, 4]` and `[11, 13, 9, 10]`.

questions.text: Given an array A with the following nodes: 12, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15. After applying the max-heapify at node 2 (30) the result will be :
questions.explanation:
Max-heapify at index 2 (value 30):
1. Children are index 4 (50) and index 5 (60).
2. The largest is 60. Swap 30 and 60. Array: `[12, 60, 40, 50, 30, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15]`.
3. Check index 5 (value 30). Children are index 10 (13) and 11 (15). Since 30 is already larger than both, the process terminates.

questions.text: The height \( h(x) \) of node \( x \) compared to its black-height \( bh(x) \) in a red black tree is:
questions.explanation: A property of Red-Black trees is that for any node \( x \), the height \( h(x) \) is at most twice the black-height \( bh(x) \). Formally: \( bh(x) \leq h(x) \leq 2bh(x) \).

questions.text: Let \( T(n) \) be a function obeying the recurrence \( T(n) = 9T(n/9) + \log n \) , then \( T(n) = \)
questions.explanation:
Applying Master Theorem: \( a=9, b=9, f(n)=\log n \).
Compute \( n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_9 9} = n^1 \).
Since \( f(n) = \log n = O(n^{1-\epsilon}) \), this is Case 1. Thus, \( T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a}) = \Theta(n) \).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the successor of node 45?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nil nodes.
**Tree Structure:**
- Root: 30 (black)
- 30's Left: 15 (black)
- 30's Right: 45 (red)
- 45's Left: 35 (black)
- 45's Right: 60 (black)
- 60's Left: 55 (red)
questions.explanation: In a Binary Search Tree (BST), the successor of a node with a right subtree is the node with the minimum value in that right subtree. The right subtree of node 45 is rooted at 60. Within this subtree, we follow the left child pointers to find the minimum value, which leads us to node 55. Therefore, the successor of 45 is 55.

questions.text: Given a heap that contains 256 nodes, the height of the heap rooted at level 5 is:
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree (heap) with \( n = 256 \) nodes has a total height of \( H = \lfloor \log_2(256) \rfloor = 8 \). If the root is at level 0, a node at level 5 has a height equal to the maximum distance to a leaf, which is \( 8 - 5 = 3 \).

questions.text:
What is the running time of the following pseudo code in Big-Oh notation:
```cpp
int XXX(int n)
{
if(n < 6)
cout << "too small";
else
for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i)
{
cout << "just right";
}
return n;
}

void main()
{
for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i)
a = XXX(i);
}
```
questions.explanation: The function `XXX(i)` has a running time of \( O(i) \) because of the loop that runs \( i \) times. The `main` function calls `XXX(i)` inside another loop that runs from 0 to \( n-1 \). The total time complexity is \( \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} i = \frac{(n-1)n}{2} \), which is \( O(n^2) \).

questions.text: Which traversal method outputs the data in sorted order in a binary search tree?
questions.explanation: Inorder traversal visits the left subtree, then the root, then the right subtree. In a Binary Search Tree (BST), this visiting order guaranteed to output elements in non-decreasing (sorted) order.

questions.text: Given the recurrence relation \( T(n) = n^2 + 2T(n/3) \), using the recursion tree method, what is the number of nodes in the tree at level \( i = 5 \)?
questions.explanation: In the recursion tree for \( T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) \), the number of nodes at level \( i \) is \( a^i \). Here, \( a = 2 \). For level \( i = 5 \), the number of nodes is \( 2^5 = 32 \).

questions.text: A complete binary search tree has a worst case height of:
questions.explanation: A 'complete' binary tree is by definition balanced. Even in the worst case, its height is always logarithmic relative to the number of nodes, specifically \( \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor \). While a standard BST can degenerate into a linked list with height \( O(n) \), a 'complete' one cannot.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the black height (bh) of node 45?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nil nodes.
**Tree Structure:**
- Root: 30 (black)
- 30's Left: 15 (black)
- 30's Right: 45 (red)
- 45's Left: 35 (black)
- 45's Right: 60 (black)
- 60's Left: 55 (red)
questions.explanation: The black height of a node \( x \) is the number of black nodes on any path from \( x \) to a leaf, not including \( x \) itself. Paths from 45 go through 35 (black) and then to a NIL leaf (black), or through 60 (black), then 55 (red), and then to a NIL leaf (black). In both cases, there are exactly 2 black nodes on the path to the leaf. Thus, \( bh(45) = 2 \).

questions.text: Insert the following elements into an initially empty min-binary heap: \( <10, 12, 1, 14, 6, 5, 8, 15, 3, 9, 7, 4, 11, 13, 2> \). What data element is sitting in the right child of the left child of the root?
questions.explanation: Building the min-heap step-by-step: After all insertions and heapifications, the array representation of the min-heap is: `[1, 3, 2, 6, 7, 5, 4, 15, 14, 12, 9, 10, 11, 13, 8]`. The root is 1 (index 0). Its left child is 3 (index 1). The children of the left child are at index \( 2 \times 1 + 1 = 3 \) and \( 2 \times 1 + 2 = 4 \). The right child of the left child is at index 4, which contains the value 7.

questions.text: Given the array \( A = \{13, 10, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6\} \), after running the loop in the insertion sort algorithm five times (for \( i=1 \) to \( 5 \)), the result will be:

Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, what is the depth of node 30?
Note 1: a node in bold is black and an underlined node is red.
Note 2: Don't forget nil nodes.
**Tree Structure:**
- Root: 30 (black)
- 30's Left: 15 (black)
- 30's Right: 45 (red)
- 45's Left: 35 (black)
- 45's Right: 60 (black)
- 60's Left: 55 (red)
questions.explanation: The depth of a node is defined as the number of edges from the root to that node. Since node 30 is the root of the tree, its depth is 0.

questions.text:
Based on the provided Red-Black tree (where **30** is the root (B), **15**(B) and 45(R) are its children, **35**(B) and **60**(B) are children of 45, and 55(R) is the left child of 60): Which case will be invoked when we insert node 50 into the tree?
(Note: bold nodes are black, underlined are red, and don't forget NIL nodes)
questions.explanation: When node 50 is inserted (as a red node), it becomes the left child of 55. 55 is red, and its parent is 60 (black). However, wait, the grandparent of 50 is 60. The uncle of 50 (right child of 60) is NIL (Black). In a standard RB-tree insertion, if the parent is red (55) and the uncle is black (NIL), and the path is linear (50 is left of 55, 55 is left of 60), Case 3 (Right Rotation on grandparent) is triggered.

questions.text: Based on the Red-Black tree described: Which case will be invoked when we insert node 10 into the tree?
questions.explanation: Node 10 would be inserted as a child of node 15. Since node 15 is black, no red-red violation occurs, so no rebalancing cases are invoked.

questions.text: Based on the Red-Black tree described: What is the height of the tree?
questions.explanation: The height is the maximum number of edges from the root to a leaf. The longest path is 30 \(\rightarrow\) 45 \(\rightarrow\) 60 \(\rightarrow\) 55, which consists of 3 edges.

questions.text: List the keys of the nodes using the pre-order traversal.
questions.explanation: Pre-order traversal visits the root, then the left subtree, then the right subtree. Following this: 30 (root) \(\rightarrow\) 15 (left) \(\rightarrow\) 45 (right) \(\rightarrow\) 35 (left of 45) \(\rightarrow\) 60 (right of 45) \(\rightarrow\) 55 (left of 60).

questions.text: Based on the Red-Black tree described: What is the successor of node 45?
questions.explanation: The in-order successor is the smallest node in the right subtree. The right subtree of 45 is rooted at 60. The leftmost (smallest) node in that subtree is 55.

questions.text: Based on the Red-Black tree described: What is the black height (bh) of node 45?
questions.explanation: Black height is the number of black nodes on a path from the node to a NIL leaf, not counting the node itself. Path from 45: 45 \(\rightarrow\) 35(B) \(\rightarrow\) NIL(B) (2 black nodes) OR 45 \(\rightarrow\) 60(B) \(\rightarrow\) 55(R) \(\rightarrow\) NIL(B) (2 black nodes: 60 and NIL).

questions.text: Based on the Red-Black tree described: What is the depth of node 30?
questions.explanation: The depth is the number of edges from the root to the node. Since node 30 is the root, its depth is 0.

questions.text: The array \{25, 18, 19, 17, 14, 16, 10, 13, 21, 5, 12, 11, 7\} is not a max-heap. but the max-heap properties are violated only once (involving numbers). Which two numbers (parent and child) in the array violate the heap properties?
questions.explanation:
In a max-heap stored in an array (using 1-based indexing), the parent of the node at index \(i\) is at index \(\lfloor i/2 \rfloor\).
The number 21 is at index 9. Its parent is at index \(\lfloor 9/2 \rfloor = 4\).
The value at index 4 is 17. Since \(17 < 21\), the max-heap property (Parent \(\ge\) Child) is violated.

Since both \(f(n)\) and \(g(n)\) are constants, they are asymptotically equal: \(f(n) = \Theta(g(n))\). This implies that both the Big-O and Big-Omega conditions are satisfied. Note: The options in the image repeat \(O(g(n))\) and use a zero typo, but choice C 'All' encompasses the valid asymptotic relationships.

questions.text: Given the array A \(<11, 10, 1, 6, 4, 13, 9, 7>\) what are the two sub arrays generated after applying the partition procedure in the Quick sort algorithm one time :
questions.explanation: Using the Lomuto partition scheme with the last element (7) as the pivot, the array is rearranged such that all elements smaller than 7 are to its left and larger elements are to its right. The sequence [1, 6, 4, 7, 11, 13, 9, 10] represents a state where 7 is the pivot, with the sub-arrays [1, 6, 4] and [11, 13, 9, 10] formed around it.

questions.text: Given an array A with the following nodes: 12, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15. After applying the max-heapify at node 2 (30) the result will be :
questions.explanation: Starting at index 2 (value 30), its children are at index 4 (50) and index 5 (60). The largest is 60. We swap 30 and 60. The value 30 is now at index 5. Its children are at index 10 (13) and index 11 (15). Since 30 is larger than both, the process stops. The resulting array is: 12, 60, 40, 50, 30, 70, 80, 90, 11, 13, 15.

questions.text: The height \(h(x)\) of node \(x\) compared to its black-height \(bh(x)\) in a red black tree is :
questions.explanation: A fundamental property of Red-Black Trees is that the height \(h(x)\) of any node is at most twice its black-height \(bh(x)\), i.e., \(bh(x) \le h(x) \le 2bh(x)\).

questions.text: Let \(T(n)\) be a function obeying the recurrence \(T(n) = 9T(n/9) + \log n\), then \(T(n) =\)
questions.explanation:
Using the Master Theorem: \(a=9, b=9, f(n) = \log n\).
\(n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_9 9} = n^1 = n\).
Since \(f(n) = \log n\) is polynomially smaller than \(n\) (i.e., \(f(n) = O(n^{1-\epsilon})\)), this falls under Case 1.
Thus, \(T(n) = \Theta(n)\), which is also \(O(n)\).

questions.text: Given a heap that contains 256 nodes, the height of the heap rooted at level 5 is :
questions.explanation: A heap with 256 nodes is a nearly complete binary tree. Its total height \(H = \lfloor \log_2(256) \rfloor = 8\) (with root at level 0). The height of a sub-tree rooted at level \(k\) is approximately \(H - k\). For \(k=5\), height is \(8 - 5 = 3\).

questions.text: Which traversal method outputs the data in sorted order in binary search tree?
questions.explanation: Inorder traversal visits nodes in the order: Left Subtree \(\rightarrow\) Root \(\rightarrow\) Right Subtree. For a Binary Search Tree (BST), this results in visiting nodes in non-decreasing (sorted) order.

questions.text:
What is the running time of the following pseudo code in Big-Oh notation :
```cpp
int XXX(int n) {
if(n < 6)
cout << "too small";
else
for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
cout << "just right";
}
return n;
}

void main() {
for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i)
a = XXX(i);
}
```
questions.explanation: The outer loop in `main` runs \(n\) times. In each iteration \(i\), it calls `XXX(i)`. The function `XXX(i)` contains a loop that runs \(i\) times. The total number of operations is proportional to \(\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} i = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}\), which is \(O(n^2)\).

questions.text: Given the recurrence relation \(T(n) = n * n + 2 * T(n/3)\). and using the recursion tree method. what is the number of nodes in the tree at level i=5?
questions.explanation: The recurrence \(T(n) = n^2 + 2T(n/3)\) indicates that each node in the recursion tree splits into 2 children. This is a binary tree structure. The number of nodes at level \(i\) (where the root is level 0) is given by \(2^i\). For level \(i=5\), the number of nodes is \(2^5 = 32\).

questions.text: A complete binary search tree has a worst case height of:
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree with \( n \) nodes always has a height of \( \lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor \). Since the structure is constrained to be complete (all levels filled except possibly the last), the height remains logarithmic even in the worst case for a binary search tree.

questions.text: Insert the following elements into an initially empty binary heap: \( <10, 12, 1, 14, 6, 5, 8, 15, 3, 9, 7, 4, 11, 13, 2> \). What data element is sitting in the right child of the left child of the root?
questions.explanation:
Using a min-heap structure and array representation (where the root is at index 1, the left child at \( 2i \), and the right child at \( 2i+1 \)):
1. After inserting all elements, the heap array is: `[1, 3, 2, 6, 7, 5, 4, 15, 14, 12, 9, 10, 11, 13, 8]`
2. Root is index 1 (value 1).
3. Left child of root is index 2 (value 3).
4. Right child of that left child is at index \( 2 \times 2 + 1 = 5 \).
5. The value at index 5 is 7.

questions.text: Given the array \( A = \{13, 10, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6\} \) after running the loop in the insertion sort algorithm five times the result will be:
questions.explanation:
Insertion sort builds the sorted list one element at a time by iterating through the array (starting from index 1):
Initial: `[13, 10, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]`
Pass 1 (item 10): `[10, 13, 4, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]`
Pass 2 (item 4): `[4, 10, 13, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]`
Pass 3 (item 19): `[4, 10, 13, 19, 2, 30, 11, 6]`
Pass 4 (item 2): `[2, 4, 10, 13, 19, 30, 11, 6]`
Pass 5 (item 30): `[2, 4, 10, 13, 19, 30, 11, 6]`
